coredata.dc:title: Catalyst-regulated conversion of p-phenylenediamine-based carbon dots from red to deep-blue emission
coredata.dc:creator: He, Pinyi
Bai, Jianliang
Qin, Fu
Wang, Xinyu
Yu, Xu
Yao, Yongkang
Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: Deep-blue emission (＜450 nm) is significant in high-density information storage, solid-state lighting, and vivid displays. However, the carbon dots (CDs) obtained from p-phenylenediamine (pPD) have only red fluorescence, which greatly limits the application and development of pPD-based CDs. As we all know, a catalyst is a substance that can change the rate and pathway of a chemical reaction. Herein, we designed to add a catalyst to realize the conversion of pPD-based CDs from red to deep-blue fluorescence. The emission peak of obtained deep-blue CDs was 440 nm, and the full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) was only 58 nm, with a high color purity. In addition, through in-depth analysis of microstructure and photoluminescence (PL) properties, it is found that the decrease of the sp2-conjugated domain and the graphite N content and the increase in pyridinic N content were the main reasons for the blue shift of the spectra. Based on this, the formation process and mechanism of red and deep-blue CDs were further elucidated. This work not only efficiently realized the design of the deep-blue CDs but also provided a new idea for the luminescence regulation of CDs without changing the raw materials and reaction conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Catalyst
Deep-blue
Luminescence regulation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275035).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PL 3D spectra of (a) red and (d) deep-blue CDs under excitation of different wavelengths. Insets are the corresponding photographs of CD solution in daylight (left) and under a UV lamp (right). Normalized UV–vis absorption, Ex, and Em spectra of (b) red and (e) deep-blue CDs. Time-resolved PL decay curves of (c) red and (f) deep-blue CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The PL spectra from red to deep-blue CDs, and the blue-shifted wavelength was up to 170 nm. (b) CIE color coordinates of red and deep-blue CDs. (c) Plot the tangent line of UV–vis spectra to find the absorption edge. (d) Illustration of the band gap transitions of red and deep-blue CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

TEM and HRTEM images (inset) of (a) red and (b) deep-blue CDs. Particle size distribution diagrams of (c) red and (d) deep-blue CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) FTIR spectra of red and deep-blue CDs. (b) Raman spectra of red and deep-blue CDs. (c) Full scan XPS spectra of red and deep-blue CDs. (d–f) High-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s XPS spectra of red CDs. (g–i) High-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s XPS spectra of deep-blue CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(1) The possible formation process of red CDs. (2) The possible formation process of deep-blue CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of the preparation of red and deep-blue CDs.

head.ce:title: Catalyst-regulated conversion of p-phenylenediamine-based carbon dots from red to deep-blue emission

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 211189, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 211189, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Deep-blue emission (＜450 nm) is significant in high-density information storage, solid-state lighting, and vivid displays. However, the carbon dots (CDs) obtained from p-phenylenediamine (pPD) have only red fluorescence, which greatly limits the application and development of pPD-based CDs. As we all know, a catalyst is a substance that can change the rate and pathway of a chemical reaction. Herein, we designed to add a catalyst to realize the conversion of pPD-based CDs from red to deep-blue fluorescence. The emission peak of obtained deep-blue CDs was 440 nm, and the full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) was only 58 nm, with a high color purity. In addition, through in-depth analysis of microstructure and photoluminescence (PL) properties, it is found that the decrease of the sp2-conjugated domain and the graphite N content and the increase in pyridinic N content were the main reasons for the blue shift of the spectra. Based on this, the formation process and mechanism of red and deep-blue CDs were further elucidated. This work not only efficiently realized the design of the deep-blue CDs but also provided a new idea for the luminescence regulation of CDs without changing the raw materials and reaction conditions.

Catalyst regulates p-phenylenediamine-based carbon dots for achieving deep-blue emission with narrow full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) and high color-purity.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon-based luminescent nanoparticles, have been widely concerned by the academic community because of their small size (usually less than 10 nm) and rich functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, etc.). With the deepening of research, scientists have discovered that CDs have lots of advantages such as simple preparation, low cost, good biocompatibility, easy modification, and excellent chemical stability [1–8], which makes this fascinating material shine in the fields of optoelectronic devices, bioimaging, catalysis, sensing, anti-counterfeiting, and batteries [9–17].

There are many kinds of raw materials for synthesizing CDs, and p-phenylenediamine (pPD) has become one of the most popular raw materials because of its conjugated aromatic structure and highly active amino groups. At present, most of the obtained pPD-based CDs are concentrated in the red fluorescent region. For instance, Jiang et al. used pPD as a raw material to get red CDs with an emission wavelength of 604 nm by a one-step solvothermal method [18]. Wang et al. treated the ethanol/H2O solution of pPD by a microwave-assisted method to obtain red CDs at 615 nm [19]. Sun et al. developed a simple solvent-free method to achieve red emission CDs by high-temperature treatment of pPD powder [20]. The above work obtained red CDs with similar emission wavelengths through three completely different synthetic methods. However, pPD-based CDs are difficult to achieve deep-blue emission with a single raw material source, which greatly limits its application and development in the field of high-density information storage, solid-state lighting, and vivid displays.

Some researchers introduced nitrogen and sulfur sources (urea, thiourea, etc.) to interact with pPD molecules and finally realized deep-blue CDs. Ding and co-workers mixed pPD and urea and performed solvothermal treatment to obtain a CD mixture, which was then purified by silica gel column chromatography to get a small amount of deep-blue CDs [21]. Bai et al. introduced thiourea into the pPD reaction system, and obtained deep-blue CDs with an emission wavelength of 445 nm by a solvothermal method [22]. Zheng et al. achieved deep-blue CDs with the same emission wavelength by adjusting the reaction molar ratio of asparagine and pPD [23]. The above works have effectively realized pPD-based deep-blue CDs by adding different reactants. However, it is hard to realize this effect with a single raw material source due to many influencing factors and complex preparation process. It is not conducive to the large-scale preparation of CDs and the in-depth exploration of the luminescence control mechanism. Therefore, how to realize pPD-based deep-blue CDs without changing the raw materials and reaction conditions is still a thorny problem.

Bai and co-workers [22] have pointed out that pPD-based red CDs are a large conjugated polymer, and the regulation of the polymerization process and conjugated structure may change its photoluminescence (PL) properties. So, how do we change the polymerization process and conjugated structure of CDs with a single raw material source? Catalysts can provide certain possibilities for this. It is known that catalysts can effectively change the rate of chemical reactions and select chemical reaction paths. Our research group has also reported the related work. We used the electron-withdrawing effect of the catalyst to activate the amino functional groups at adjacent positions of the oPD molecule, break the hydrogen bond interaction between the amino groups, weaken the steric effect of the oPD molecule, and promote the formation of complexes with large conjugated structures. Finally, red CDs with large sp2-conjugated domains and high graphitic N content were obtained in the further polymerization and carbonization processes, achieving the conversion of yellow to red fluorescence [24]. Therefore, choosing a suitable catalyst may play a role in regulating the polymerization process of CDs and then realizing the tunability of the conjugated structure and PL spectra. Unlike oPD molecules, the two amino functional groups of pPD molecules are in opposite positions, without hydrogen bonding interactions and steric effects, so it is easier to polymerize under high temperature and pressure to obtain large-size conjugated structures [20,25]. In this case, the introduction of a catalyst in this reaction system may change the original polymerization process of pPD molecules, resulting in changes in microstructure and optical properties. Based on this, we used pPD as the only raw material, designed AlCl3 as the catalyst, regulated the polymerization process and conjugation structure of pPD-based CDs through the electron-withdrawing effect of metal cations, and finally realized the conversion of red to high color-purity deep-blue CDs (Scheme 1 ).

pPD was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. AlCl3·6H2O was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Anhydrous ethanol was purchased from Wuxi Yasheng Chemical Co., Ltd. The reagents used in the experiments were all analytical grade without further purification.

First, 0.6 g pPD was fully dissolved in 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol, and then the solution was put into a 50 mL Teflon reactor and reacted in an oven at 200 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, it was cooled to room temperature naturally, and the obtained crude product emitted red under a UV lamp. Subsequently, the crude product was separated and purified by silica gel column chromatography using ethanol as the eluent to remove small size impurities. Finally, CD powder was obtained by rotary evaporation and vacuum drying with a yield of 40.3 %.

First, 0.6 g pPD and 0.01786 g AlCl3·6H2O were fully dissolved in 30 ml of anhydrous ethanol solution, and then the mixed solution was placed in a 50 ml Teflon reactor and reacted at 200 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, it was cooled to room temperature naturally, and the obtained crude product emitted deep-blue under a UV lamp. Subsequently, the crude product was separated and purified by silica gel column chromatography using ethanol as the eluent to obtain the CDs with bright deep-blue fluorescence. Under the elution of the eluent and the adsorption of silica gel powder, the catalyst and other impurities were well removed, achieving effective separation of catalysts and deep-blue CDs. Finally, CD powder was obtained by rotary evaporation and vacuum drying with a yield of 30.2 %.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained by a Talos F200X microscope operating at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were identified using a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer with dried KBr pellets. Raman spectra were collected by a Thermo Scientific DXR spectrometer with the excitation wavelength of 532 nm laser. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) curves were characterized on a Shimadzu/Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD instrument. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were tested with a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were performed on a SHIMADZU RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer. The absolute PLQY and time-resolved PL spectra were recorded by a PluoroLog 3-TCSPC steady transient fluorescence spectrometer equipped with the integrating sphere.

Before studying the optical properties and structural composition of red and deep-blue CDs, we purified them by strict silica gel column chromatography. Under the elution of the eluent and the adsorption of silica gel powder, the catalyst and other impurities were well removed, achieving effective separation of catalysts and CDs. Subsequently, the optical characteristics and fluorescence sources of the two CDs were compared and analyzed. From the PL spectra, as the excitation wavelength gradually increased from 420 to 580 nm, the emission peak of red CDs was fixed at 610 nm with a full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of 85 nm, showing excitation-independent fluorescence properties (Fig. 1 (a) and Fig. S1(a)). The inset was a picture of the CDs solution, which emitted red fluorescence under a UV lamp. Unlike red CDs, the spectra of deep-blue CDs were fixed at 440 nm, with the FWHM of only 58 nm, suggesting a high color purity (Fig. 1(d) and Fig. S1(b)). The inset picture showed bright deep-blue fluorescence under a UV lamp. The fluorescence emission wavelength of the two CDs does not change with the excitation wavelength, which indicates that they have a single fluorescence emission source [26]. From the UV–vis absorption spectra, peaks less than 300 nm are attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC bond. Compared with the absorption peak of deep-blue CDs at 300–400 nm, red CDs showed obvious absorption signals at 400–500 nm, indicating that red CDs might have extended conjugated structures while deep-blue CDs had smaller conjugated structures (Fig. 1(b)(e) and Fig. S2) [27–30]. In addition, the PL decay curves of red CDs were fitted by double-exponential functions with an average lifetime of 4.05 ns (Fig. 1(c) and Table S3), indicating it has two relaxation channels, in which the short lifetime ( τ
1) represents the fluorescence may originate from the radiative recombination of carbon core states, the long lifetime ( τ
2) is attributed to the radiative recombination of surface states. In contrast, the curves of deep-blue CDs can be well fitted by a mono-exponential function with an average lifetime of 9.71 ns (Fig. 1(f) and Table S3), verifying the fluorescence has only one relaxation process, and the carbon core state dominates the PL emission [31–33]. Furthermore, we tested the absolute PLQY to study the fluorescence performance of two CDs. Among them, the absolute PLQY values of red and deep-blue CDs are 9.23 % and 16.73 %, respectively (Fig. S3). The test results show that the deep-blue CDs achieved by the catalyst regulation strategy have better fluorescence properties.

From the analysis of the above spectral characteristics, the peak of the PL spectra was tuned from 610 to 440 nm, and the wavenumbers moving from red to deep-blue area were as high as 170 nm (Fig. 2 (a)). According to the calculation results of CIE color coordinates, the spectra of CDs are transformed from the red (0.64, 0.36) to the deep-blue region (0.15, 0.08) (Fig. 2(b)), the remarkable luminescence regulation confirms the effectiveness of the catalyst regulation strategy. The color coordinates of the designed deep-blue CDs were very close to the standard color Rec. 2020 (0.131, 0.046) specification [34], which proved that the obtained deep-blue CDs has excellent optical properties. Furthermore, we determined the band gaps between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LOMO) energy levels by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the tangent lines were drawn at the abrupt points of the absorption spectra, and the absorption edges (λedge) of CDs were determined to be 607 and 382 nm. According to the equation Egap = 1240/λedge, the band gaps of red and deep-blue CDs were calculated to be 2.04 and 3.25 eV (Fig. 2(d)) [24,35,36]. The results showed that the increase in band gaps matched the blue shift of PL spectra.

To observe the micromorphology and structure of red and deep-blue CDs, we conducted TEM and HRTEM characterizations. From Fig. 3 (a) and (b), both CDs were quasi-spherical structures with relatively uniform dispersion and narrow particle size distribution, and obvious lattice fringes were observed. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [37]. It shows that both red and deep-blue CDs have a certain crystallinity. According to particle size statistics, the average sizes of red and deep-blue CDs were 9.95 and 6.62 nm (Fig. 3(c) and (d)), indicating the blue shift of PL spectra may be related to the quantum size effect [25,37]. Further, FTIR, Raman, and XPS spectra were used to analyze CDs' surface functional groups and elemental composition. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the absorption peak at 1629 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CO/CN bonds and the signal at 1513 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of aromatic ring CC bond. From the comparison of the relative intensities of the above two signal peaks, it can be concluded that the content of CC relatively decreased while the CO/CN content relatively increased during the change from red to deep-blue CDs, indicating that deep-blue CDs may have less sp2-conjugated carbon and more pyridinic N structure, which is consistent with the changing trend of the conjugated domains analyzed in the UV–vis absorption spectra. The characteristic peaks at 1265 and 1126 cm−1 own to the stretching vibrations of C–N and C–O bonds, respectively. The signal peaks in the 2800-3000 cm−1 region were attributed to the Ar–H and –CH3/–CH2– groups and the broad signal peak at 3436 cm−1 proved the existence of –OH/-NH2 groups, verifying that both CDs have good water solubility. The signal peaks of deep-blue CDs are relatively strong, verifying that they may have more –OH/-NH2 functional groups. The presence of CC, CN, and C–N groups suggested that both CDs formed polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon structures during the reaction process [21,36,38–42]. In addition, no bonding of the Al element was found in the FTIR spectra, proving that AlCl3 was not doped into the structure of deep-blue CDs. In the Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 4(b)), the D and G bands refer to the disordered and graphitic structures of carbon materials. The intensity of the G-band is obviously stronger than that of the D-band, and the IG/ID values of red and deep-blue CDs were 1.06 and 1.05, indicating the two prepared CDs had a certain degree of graphitization [21,38,43,44]. There are several weak signal peaks in the range of 1100–1250 cm−1, which are caused by the existence of the aromatic C–N heterocyclic structure [45,46]. From the Full scan XPS spectra (Fig. 4(c)), both CDs have the same element composition, in which the content of C was as high as 80.30 % and 82.22 %, the content of N was 5.50 % and 7.17 %, and the content of O was 14.20 % and 10.60 % (Table S1). The high content of the C element proved that two CDs have a high carbonization degree, the presence of the O element indicated that anhydrous ethanol participates in the polymerization and carbonization process, and groups such as hydroxyl groups were bonded to the surface of CDs. With the blue shift of the PL spectra, the content of the N element increased, while the content of the O element decreased, suggesting the content of N groups and the degree of oxidation of CDs are the factors that affect the PL characteristics. Similarly, no bonding of the Al element was found in the XPS spectra, which proved that AlCl3 only affects the formation and polymerization process, and is not doped or left in the structure of deep-blue CDs. Subsequently, high-resolution XPS spectra were used to compare and analyze the bonding of different elements. The C1s spectra of red CDs have characteristic peaks at 284.48, 285.03, 286.19, and 288.76 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–N, C–O, and CO (Fig. 4(d)). The N1s spectra displayed three signal peaks owning to pyridine N (398.79 eV), amino N (399.07 eV), and graphite N (400.01 eV) (Fig. 4(e)). The high-resolution O1s spectra have signal peaks of CO and C–O bonds (531.68 and 533.05 eV) (Fig. 4(f)). Similar to the above analysis results, the C1s spectra of deep-blue CDs showed four signal peaks at 284.34 (C–C/CC), 285.07 (C–N), 286.70 (C–O), and 288.98 eV (CO) (Fig. 4(g)). The N1s spectra have three characteristic signals corresponding to pyridine N, amino N, and graphite N (398.50, 399.01, and 400.41 eV) (Fig. 4(h)). The high-resolution O1s spectra have signal peaks of CO (531.60 eV) and C–O (533.34 eV) (Fig. 4(i)) [47–51]. To intuitively compare the types and contents of different element bonds, we made a comparison table of the relative contents of surface functional groups (Table S2). Through the comparative analysis between Tables S1 and S2, it can be found that during the conversion of red to deep-blue CDs, the content of C–C/CC and graphite N all went down, while the pyridine N content obviously increased. It is concluded that the reduction of the sp2-conjugated domain of the carbon core and the decrease in graphite N content, as well as the increase in pyridine N content, are the main reasons for the blue shift of PL spectra.

Through the above comparative analysis, the catalyst AlCl3 significantly regulated the structure and PL properties of pPD-based CDs. The possible formation process and mechanism were further analyzed by the results of structure and composition. First, the two amino functional groups of pPD molecules are at opposite positions and have less interaction with each other, so they have less steric hindrance and higher reactivity. In the absence of a catalyst, pPD molecules will self-polymerize in an ethanol solution and gradually polycondensation to form a long-chain polymer [51]. Due to the contribution of anhydrous ethanol to the reaction process, CO and O–H groups will be bonded on the long-chain polymer. With the increase of reaction time, the long-chain polymers were crosslinked together through the dehydrogenation process, and in-situ carbonization occurred at the same time, resulting in a large sp2-conjugated domain, high graphitic N and less pyridine N content structure. Finally, the red CDs were formed (Fig. 5 (1)) [20,25]. Different from the formation process of red CDs, under the attraction of metal cations to the amino lone pair electrons, the original long-chain polymerization process of pPD molecules will inevitably be destroyed. Therefore, the pPD molecules are more likely to form short-chain polymers in ethanol solution under the electron-withdrawing effect of catalyst AlCl3. Since the anhydrous ethanol participated in the reaction, groups such as –OH and CO were bonded to the edge of the short-chain polymers. As the reaction proceeds, the short-chain polymers were closely combined through the dehydrogenation process, and the in-situ carbonization process occurred. Finally, the carbon cores with less graphitic N content, high pyridine N content, and small sp2-conjugated domains were obtained (Fig. 5(2)). Since graphitic N is an electron-donating element, it can generate an intermediate band gap and play a role in narrowing the optical band gaps. Therefore, the decrease in graphitic N content will lead to the increase of optical band gaps, which will cause the blue shift of PL spectra. Similarly, the increase in the pyridine N content may lead to exciton capture and increase structural defects, thereby affecting the blue shift of fluorescence. The reduction of the sp2-conjugated domain of the carbon core will also promote the change of the luminescence to a shorter wavelength area [5,52–54]. Finally, the deep-blue fluorescence was realized under the synergistic effect of pyridine N, graphitic N, and conjugated domains.

In summary, we have successfully designed a simple and efficient method for regulating the luminescence of CDs with a catalyst, which can realize the conversion of CDs from red to deep-blue without changing the raw materials and reaction conditions. In the process of polymerization and dehydrogenation, the metal cation captures the lone pair electrons of the amino group, destroys the long-chain polymerization process of the pPD molecules, and obtains the structure of short-chain polymerization. Therefore, AlCl3 plays an important role in controlling the size of the sp2-conjugated domain, and the content of graphitic N and pyridine N, realizing the tunability of the optical band gaps and PL spectra of CDs. The emission peak of the designed deep-blue CDs is fixed at 440 nm with a high color purity (FWHM = 58 nm). The blue-shifted wavelengths are up to 170 nm, and the color coordinates of deep-blue CDs are (0.15, 0.08), which is very close to the standard color Rec. 2020 (0.131, 0.046) specification. This work not only provides new insights into the effective realization of luminescence regulation of CDs but also sets the stage for designing and developing deep-blue materials.

Pinyi He: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jianliang Bai: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fu Qin: Software, Resources. Xinyu Wang: Supervision, Formal analysis. Xu Yu: Resources, Investigation. Yongkang Yao: Resources. Lili Ren: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275035).
